coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots derived from human hair for ppb level chloroform sensing in water
coredata.dc:creator: Singh, Amandeep
Eftekhari, Ehsan
Scott, James
Kaur, Jasreet
Yambem, Soniya
Leusch, Frederic
Wellings, Rodney
Gould, Tim
Ostrikov, Kostya
Sonar, Prashant
Li, Qin
coredata.dc:description: Chlorination is a widely adopted disinfection method in water and wastewater treatment for the protection of public health, as it greatly reduces pathogen risks and associated incidence of waterborne diseases. Chlorination, however, also creates disinfection by-products (DBPs) such as trihalomethanes, which, epidemiologically, have been associated with a higher incidence of some forms of cancer. Therefore, developing sensors for monitoring the chlorine dosage and DBPs concentrations in real-time and in-line is of critical importance to public health. Carbon dots (CDs) are an emergent class of fluorescent nanomaterial offering highly-sensitive sensing functionalities towards a diverse range of chemical/biochemical contaminants, owing to their tuneable fluorescence, rich surface functionalities, low to non-toxicity and ease of synthesis. In this study, we demonstrate that 1) highly fluorescent CDs (quantum yield 38%) can be produced from simple thermal treatment from biowastes such as human hairs without employing any solvents; 2) the fluorescence spectra and intensity of the synthesized CDs responds to the presence and quantity of chloroform with high sensitivity, with a limit of detection of 3 ppb; and 3) through a pair-wise comparative study on autoclave-synthesized CDs (OCDs) and microwave-synthesized counterpart, we discovered that the pyridinic N oxide that is unique to OCDs imparts sensitive and selective sensing functionality towards chloroform; 4) the PL-based sensing functionality is not solely determined by the binding affinity between the analyte and the carbon dots, but also the electronic structures of the interacting entities. This study provides an in-depth study on how to utilize the diverse features of biowastes and impart unique functionalities to CDs for developing advanced functional materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence-based sensing
Chloroform sensing
Hair valorisation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots from human hair
Synthesis of CDs in an autoclave (OCD)
Synthesis of CDs via microwave oven (MCD)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Australian Research Council Industry Transformational Research Hub

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: A.S. acknowledges QUT for the postgraduate research award. The data reported in this work was obtained at the Central Analytical Research Facility (CARF) operated by the Institute for Future Environments at QUT. Prashant Sonar is thankful to QUT for financial support from the Australian Research Council (ARC) for the Future Fellowship ( FT130101337 ) and QUT core funding ( QUT/322120-0301/07 ). Q.L. acknowledges the support of Australian Research Council Industry Transformational Research Hub ( IH 180100002 ). Ehsan Eftekhari acknowledges the support from the SIEF STEM+ Business Fellowship and Griffith University ECR Industry Collaborative Grant . The authors acknowledge the support from the Queensland node of the Australian National Fabrication Facility (ANFF), a company established under the National Collaborative Research Infrastructure Strategy to provide nano-and microfabrication facilities for Australia's researchers. This research was supported by the Griffith University Gowonda HPC Cluster.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of synthesis process of CDs hair filaments and uncoiling of tertiary structure of hair, (b) TEM image and size distribution of OCD, (c) TEM image and size distribution of MCD, (d) HRTEM images of OCD and (e) HRTEM images of MCD.

(a) High-resolution C1s XPS spectra of OCD. Inset XPS survey spectra of OCD (b) High-resolution N1s XPS spectra of OCD. Inset shows schematic of different ways nitrogen bonded in OCD. (c) High-resolution C1s XPS spectra of MCD. Inset XPS survey spectra of MCD. (d) High-resolution N1s XPS spectra of MCD. Inset shows schematic of different ways nitrogen bonded in MCD.
UV–Vis spectrum and photoluminescence spectra of (a) OCD and MCD (b) under 435 nm excitation.

(a) The fluorescence (FL) response of OCD with various concentrations of Chloroform. Stern-Volmer plot concentration-dependent FL linear response of the OCD by Chloroform concentrations within the range of (b) 0–0.05 ppm and (c) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of NO, CHCl3-NO, and CHCl3 (d) Comparison of different sensing work reported in the literature with this work.

(a) Displays the bound THM and CD model system and it's electron density at the first excited energy level (LUMO) (isosurface = 0.02 a.u.) for chloroform (i) and bromoform (ii). Note that the electron wavefunction is drawn onto the bromoform molecule, whilst the chloroform molecule has no shared electron density. (b) Shows the molecular orbitals of the first four excitations for chloroform (i), bromodichloromethane (ii), bromoform (iii) and dibromochloromethane (iv) (isosurface = 0.02 a.u.). Attention should be drawn to the small overlap present at the LUMO+1 level for bromodichloromethane, and LUMO level for dibromochloromethane. (c) Experimental results for the photoluminescence enhancement/quenching of various analytes, esp. THMs and similar molecules. The analytes used in the computational study can be seen in the last four columns to the right.
Binding energy of analyte to CDs.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots derived from human hair for ppb level chloroform sensing in water

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CSIRO-QUT Joint Sustainable Processes and Devices Lab, Queensland University of Technology (QUT), Brisbane, Queensland 4000, Australia

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Chemistry, and Physics, Queensland University of Technology (QUT), Brisbane, Queensland 4000, Australia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chlorination is a widely adopted disinfection method in water and wastewater treatment for the protection of public health, as it greatly reduces pathogen risks and associated incidence of waterborne diseases. Chlorination, however, also creates disinfection by-products (DBPs) such as trihalomethanes, which, epidemiologically, have been associated with a higher incidence of some forms of cancer. Therefore, developing sensors for monitoring the chlorine dosage and DBPs concentrations in real-time and in-line is of critical importance to public health. Carbon dots (CDs) are an emergent class of fluorescent nanomaterial offering highly-sensitive sensing functionalities towards a diverse range of chemical/biochemical contaminants, owing to their tuneable fluorescence, rich surface functionalities, low to non-toxicity and ease of synthesis. In this study, we demonstrate that 1) highly fluorescent CDs (quantum yield 38%) can be produced from simple thermal treatment from biowastes such as human hairs without employing any solvents; 2) the fluorescence spectra and intensity of the synthesized CDs responds to the presence and quantity of chloroform with high sensitivity, with a limit of detection of 3 ppb; and 3) through a pair-wise comparative study on autoclave-synthesized CDs (OCDs) and microwave-synthesized counterpart, we discovered that the pyridinic N oxide that is unique to OCDs imparts sensitive and selective sensing functionality towards chloroform; 4) the PL-based sensing functionality is not solely determined by the binding affinity between the analyte and the carbon dots, but also the electronic structures of the interacting entities. This study provides an in-depth study on how to utilize the diverse features of biowastes and impart unique functionalities to CDs for developing advanced functional materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Our fresh water supplies are challenged by increasing contamination due to the increased use of industrial and natural chemicals [1]. Affordable, real-time, in-line sensors that can monitor the diverse range of chemicals/biochemicals in water are in high demand. A particular problem in drinking water supply is the dilemma of chlorine disinfection. While disinfection of drinking water greatly reduced pathogen risks and associated incidences of waterborne disease and is recognized as one of the major public health accomplishments of the 20th century, the high reactivity of HClO and ClO− in chlorine disinfection with natural organic matter also induces formation of a variety of toxic disinfection by-products such as Cl−/Br−/I− trihalomethanes (THMs), including chloroform (CHCl3), which could be harmful to human and aquatic life [2]. Water authorities around the world have set safe limits for THMs, with some countries using total THMs and others providing standards for each individual THMs. For example, the Australian Drinking Water Guidelines provide a guideline value of 250 ppb for total THMs, while the World Health Organization (WHO) has set individual guidelines for each THM, including chloroform at 300 ppb. However, so far in water treatment plants, THMs are mostly measured by chromatography-based analytical techniques, which are incompatible with real-time, in-line monitoring systems. There have been some reports on fluorescence-based sensors for chloroform using thiol functionalized reduced graphene oxide [3] or plasmonic/polymer composite structures [4], however, the sensitivity of these systems is extremely low and are in the range of hundreds of ppm in gas phase. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop a sensor system for real-time monitoring of THM concentrations to achieve a healthy balance between disinfection of water supply and the production of toxic DBPs.

The application of carbon dots (CDs) in water quality sensing has received growing attention in recent years owing to their high quantum yield [5], tuneable fluorescence [6], rich surface functionalities [7,8], ease in fabrication [9], low cost [10] and none to low toxicity [11]. Although their fluorescence origin is not yet fully understood, the strong application potential of CDs including being a substitute for conventional semiconductor quantum dots is well recognized in the materials community. CDs have been widely reported for their use in bioimaging agents [12], trackable drug delivery system [13], light harvesting [14], chemical [15–18], and biochemical [19] sensors, and efficient lighting devices [20]. Common to all these studies is the innumerable choice of CDs precursors, dopants and surface functionalities.

The humble chemical composition of carbon dots suggests that they can be synthesized from biological or environmental waste, providing an exciting opportunity to convert waste into advanced functional nanomaterials for achieving a circular economy and sustainable development [21]. Various waste materials have been turned into carbon dots such as orange peels [22], fish scales [23], waste paper [24], onion waste [25] and hair [26]. The choice of precursors from waste are innumerable but human hair as the waste precursor is particularly interesting due to its easy accessibility, high stability and high protein content (predominantly keratin), which can serendipitously dope nitrogen (N) element in the CDs and vary their photoluminescence properties, such as quantum yield (QY) and enrich surface functionalities. Preceding studies have successfully demonstrated the feasibility of synthesizing carbon nanomaterials from human hairs, but with relative low QY (10–25%) [26–28]. Liu et al.26 synthesized CDs from hair strand via a pyrolysis method, which exhibited the highest PLQY around 17%. Guo and co-workers [28] demonstrated solvent-free CDs from hair with a very high synthesis yield of 95% but much lower QY of 10.75%. Hou and co-workers [29] synthesized CDs from hair in water using the hydrothermal process, resulting in a QY of 24.8%. It is worth noting that the CDs derived from hairs in the above studies all have shown sensitivity to Hg2+ in water with regards to their photoluminescence (PL) response.

In this study, we report on solvent-free green synthesis methods for preparing CDs using a microwave (MCD) and conventional thermal treatment in the autoclave (OCD), respectively. The comparison shows that the quantity of doped nitrogen and species of nitrogen-containing functional groups can be tuned via different synthesis protocols and the functionality of the resultant CDs varies based on synthesis conditions used. Human hairs from a single common source were used to synthesise nitrogen-doped CDs under controlled experiment. A small amount of nitrogen (1.21%) was doped in the case of MCD, while a relatively higher amount of nitrogen (8.91%) was doped in the case of OCD. The OCD exhibits a QY of 38% versus 17% of MCD. Distinctive to OCDs in terms of surface functional groups is the pyridine-N-oxide, which can act as a selective sensing motif for chloroform in water, the major component of THMs in most chlorine-treated drinking waters. The OCDs shows high selectivity to chloroform with the enhancement of emission peak located at 480 nm upon excitation at 360 nm, achieving the limit of detection of 3 ppb (parts per billion), the highest sensitivity reported so far in optical nanosensors. A computational study was performed to reveal this sensing probe's working principles. This study shows an innovative approach for preparing advanced functional nanomaterials from human waste such as hair, as well as the designing principles for developing CDs-based nanosensors.

Human hairs were collected from individual hair comb. Octadecane and aluminium oxide powder (average particle size less than 1 μm) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. All reagents were of analytical grade and were used without any further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots from human hair
Synthesis of CDs in an autoclave (OCD)
ce:section.ce:para: 1.7 g of human hairs were washed in copious distilled water using the sonication for 10 min, followed by washing with copious acetone and distilled water under sonication in sequence Finally, the hair sample was dried at 60 °C in a hot air oven. The dried sample was then transferred to a Teflon container and sealed inside the steel casing of the autoclave. The autoclave was kept in a hot air oven at 180 °C for 24 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the dark brown product was collected from the autoclave and sonicated in 30 ml of ultrapure water for 60 min to obtain a homogeneous suspension. After sonication, the solution was filtered with a column containing aluminium oxide particles to remove the unwanted reaction by-products.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs via microwave oven (MCD)
ce:section.ce:para: 1.7 g of human hair was washed and dried as the procedure described above. The treated sample was then transferred to a ceramic crucible and kept inside a Panasonic microwave with a power of 1100 W for 5 min. In this method, the sample was exposed to the ambient conditions while heating, whereas in OCD synthesis (above) the sample was sealed in an autoclave, and this is a major difference between the two methods. After heating human hair inside the microwave, the sample was mixed with 30 ml of water and sonicated for 60 min to obtain a uniform dispersion of CDs in water. The CDs were then purified by filtration with a column containing aluminium oxide particles to remove the unwanted reaction by-products.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of CDs were obtained using HRTEM (model JEOL 2100, 200 kV). The average size and size distribution were calculated by analysing approx. 1000 CDs. The absorption spectra of each sample were recorded using Cary 60 G6860A spectrophotometer (Agilent). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was performed using a Bruker ATR. The vibrational spectra of molecular bonds of CDs were obtained by using in Via (Renishaw) Raman spectrometer with a laser source of wavelength 785 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) experiments were performed on Axis Supra (Kratos) with aluminium Al Ka (h V = 1486.7 eV). Photoluminescence spectra of excitation and emission of both CDs were recorded on Fluorescence Spectrophotometer Eclipse G9800A (Cary). The PL decay was measured at the peak of emission (420 nm) using TCSPC capability of Horiba (DeltaFlex) lifetime fluorescence spectroscope with sample excited with pulse LED at 365 nm. For the sensing experiments, the photoluminescence spectra were recorded using Ocean Optic Spectrometer.

Detection of chloroform (CHCl3) in water was performed at room temperature. OCD and MCD solutions with a given concentration were prepared before measurement. Fifty microliters of each CD solution was transferred into a well plate followed by addition of a calculated amount of CHCl3 solution. A three minutes time interval was given to allow a good diffusion-driven mixing in the plate, before the PL measurement. The selectivity of both OCD and MCD were also evaluated against Na+, Ca2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Ag+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, NH4 +, NO3 −, SO4 2−, PO4 3−, Mo4+, Mg2+, V5+, HClO−, Cl−, tetrachloroethene, dichloroacetic acid, trichloroethylene, dichloromethane, ethanol, tetrahydrofuran (THF), acetone, bromodichloromethane, dibromochloromethane, bromoform, and the mixture of tetrachloroethene, dichloroacetic acid, trichloroethylene, dichloromethane and chloroform (mixed), all at a concentration of 1 ppm.

Computational work on the CD and THM system was conducted using the Gaussian software [30]. Due to the size of the carbon dot (tens of thousands of atoms), and hence the computational power that would be required to simulate it, simplified models were created in Avogadro [31,32]. These models consisted of a small region of sp2 hybridised carbon (8–10 aromatic rings), with a pyridinic oxide functional group on the edge; this corresponds to the proposed binding sites. There are two simplest forms of possible pyridinic oxide binding sites, armchair and zigzag edge. Along with these binding sites, four THM molecules; chloroform, bromoform, bromodichloromethane and dibromochloromethane, were simulated. The calculations were performed with the B3LYP density functional method [33–36] with the cc-pVTZ basis set [37]. The choice of B3LYP was made due to its popularity in the field, and its suitability for organic molecules. Because the OCD's are used/studied in water is it important to treat the systems in solution. To achieve this the polarizable continuum model (PCM) implicit solvation method was used [38], due to its relatively low computational costs.

Once the calculations had been completed, the molecular orbitals and optimised structures were studied. Additionally, the binding energy for each THM with the two binding sites was determined using:
E B
=
E
S + A
−
E s
+
E A
,
where E
B is the binding energy, E
S+A is the energy of the bound sensor and analyte, and E
s and E
A are the energies of the individual sensor and analyte, respectively. Making a judgement based upon the strengths of the dipoles produced by THMs, it can be predicted that chloroform will possess the highest binding energy, followed by bromodichloromethane, dibromochloromethane and finally bromoform.

The human hair-based CDs synthesis schematics using solvent-free heating in an autoclave and in the microwave, oven are shown in Fig. 1a. The thermal decomposition process involves more than one of these chemical mechanisms, (a) random chain scission, (b) chain unzipping, (c) chain stripping and (d) cross-linking depending upon the structure of precursor [39]. Since the hair precursor is stable, the thermal decomposition starts through random chain scission after uncoiling of tertiary structure of hair. Usually, the scission starts at the weakest link first, breaking disulphide bonds, hydrogen bonds and salt bonds (Fig. 1a). This leads to the generation of a large number of small hydrocarbons. The molecules with geometrical favourable structure slowly increase in quantity. Once the cleavage of the main chain is complete, the thermal degradation of weaker CC bonds occurs. In this disintegration process, the nitrogen gets released from the depolymerisation process. Finally, the recombination reaction kicks in leading to formation of CDs. The singlet oxygen generated during the depolymerisation could also assist to oxidise the CD surface [39].

The TEM images of OCD (Fig. 1b and S1b) clearly exhibit particles at a scale bar of 100 and 20 nm, respectively. The inset illustrates the narrow size distribution of OCD with a majority between 7 and 13 nm. Similarly, the TEM images at a scale bar of 200 and 100 nm for MCD are shown in Fig. 1d and S2a, respectively. The inset in Fig. 1d shows MCD has a broad size distribution ranging from 65 to 90 nm.

The HRTEM images of OCD (Fig. 1d) shows resolved lattice fringes of OCD crystal with a lattice spacing of 0.22 nm, whilst the lattice spacing of MCD is determined as 0.23 nm (Fig. 1e). This indicates that there is considerable pressure towards the centre of the particle which caused a decrease in d spacing from the normal 0.34 nm graphitic layer spacing [40]. In other words, both types of CDs possess spacing corresponding to diamond-like structure. The electron diffraction pattern of both CDs indicates a crystalline nature with a small variation in size distribution, which is shown in the inset of Fig. S1(b) and S2(b), respectively.

The chemical composition and structural analysis of both CDs were investigated by XPS, FTIR, and Raman spectroscopy techniques. The XPS survey spectra (Inset Fig. 2a) clearly show that the OCD is comprised of carbon (76.6%), oxygen (13.8%) and nitrogen (8.91%). Since OCD was synthesized in a sealed Teflon container at 180 °C, it led to high concentration nitrogen doping (8.91%) in OCD. The hair precursor itself is primarily comprised of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen. A small amount (0.65%) of sodium (Na1s, 1072 eV) was also observed in survey spectra and this is likely an impurity. The high-resolution C1s spectrum of OCD (Fig. 2a) can be fitted with three peaks: a strongest peak at 286 eV attributed to CO bond (55%), at the peak at 286.6 eV ascribed to C-O-C bond (31%) and a third (weakest) peak at 289.3 eV corresponding to CO bond (13%). The high-resolution N1s spectrum of OCD (Fig. 2b) can be fitted into two peaks: the strong peak at 401.1 eV corresponds to graphitic N (78.7%), while a small peak at 402.6 eV can be attributed to pyridinic NO bond (21.3%). The prevalence of graphitic N indicates that most of the nitrogen is directly doped into the core of OCD. It appears that the treatment of hair under elevated temperature in an enclosed system has yielded a considerable amount of pyridinic N oxide on the surface of OCD.

Similarly, a detailed XPS analysis for MCD was performed. The XPS survey spectra (inset Fig. 2c) of MCD show that carbon (84.98%), oxygen (13.81%) and nitrogen (1.21%) elements are present in MCD. Since they were synthesized in an open atmosphere using a microwave (1100 W) as a source of heating, MCD appears to possess significantly less N content in comparison to OCD, with a percentage of N content at 1.21%. The amount of oxygen on the surface of MCD (31.09%) is higher compared to OCD (13.81%). The high-resolution C1s spectrum of MCD (Fig. 2c) shows three peaks: a strong peak at 285.8 eV corresponding to the CO bond, at 286.7 eV ascribed to C-O-C bond, and a weak one at 289.5 eV corresponding to CO bond. The content of CO, C-O-C, and CO bonds are 70.3%, 22.3%, and 7.5%, respectively. Though it is known that microwave-assisted carbonization and conventional heating in the enclosed vessel could produce different materials from the same precursor, the detailed mechanisms are yet entirely understood as the temperature profile in microwave synthesis is difficult to obtain.

Nevertheless, the difference in high-resolution N1s spectra of OCD and MCD (Fig. 2d) is of particular note. The N1S of MCD can be split into two peaks: the strong peak at 401.2 eV corresponds to graphitic N, similar to OCD, and a small peak at 399.6 eV corresponding to NH bond. The content of the CN bond is 97%, while that of NH bond 3%. It is curious to note that the microwave synthesis did not facilitate the formation of pyridinic N-oxide, but allowed some retention of NH2 group, most likely inherited from the parent hair proteins.

The FTIR spectrum of the OCD (Fig. S3a) exhibits aromatic CH sharp bending vibration at 660 and 990 cm−1. The corresponding CH stretching dual-band appears at 2877 and 2950 cm−1. The small peak at 752 cm−1 corresponds to out of plane CH band vibration. The two small peaks at 848 and 926 cm−1 are due to CO stretching vibration. The CN stretching vibration peak at 1112 cm−1 and peaks at 1258, 1300 and 1500 cm−1 due to NO stretching vibration are in agreement with high resolution XPS results. The FTIR spectrum of MCD shows a significantly different profile (Fig. S3b). With detailed interpretation in the Supporting Information, herewith, we highlight the NH stretching and bending bands at 3300 and 1540 cm−1, respectively, showing the presence of an amino group that is absent in OCD.

The chemical structures of OCD and MCD were further investigated by Raman analysis (Fig. S3c). The D band is located at 1316 cm−1, which is a signature peak of sp3 orbitals vibrational frequency, whereas the sp2 orbital pushes the frequency band in Raman to 1584 cm−1 (G band). The ratio of Intensity of D and G band (I
D
/I
G ) gives information about sp3/sp2 characteristics of both CDs. The I
D
/I
G ratio of MCD is 1.14, while that of OCD is 1.12 indicating both types of nanocarbon possess a high proportion of disorder. In the case of OCD, the width of Raman peak is notably smaller than MCD, which corresponds to a higher degree of crystallinity. A small shoulder near 1200 cm−1 is attributed to a charge transfer between the π-π band between NO and OCD ring (Fig. S3c) [41].

The chemical compositional analysis is summarized in the schematics of OCD and MCD shown as insets in Fig. 2b and d. The two different thermal treatment methods to the hair samples have resulted in two sets of CDs, with not only significantly different amount of doped N (OCD, 8.91% N versus MCD, 1.21% N) but also in chemical structure. The enclosed conventional oven heating at 180 °C for 24 h has resulted in OCD with graphitic N and pyridinic N oxide, while microwave heating at 1100 W for 5 min in the open air has resulted in MCD with graphitic N and analinic N.

We further investigated the optical properties such as absorbance and emission behaviour of synthesized CDs from human hair. Both OCDs and MCDs show strong absorbance in the UV range. In the case of OCD (Fig. 3a), a weak absorption peak near 280 nm is observed, which may be attributed to π ➔ π* transition corresponding to the pyridine N oxide group [41]. In comparison, the UV–vis spectrum of MCDs (Fig. 3b), presents a distinctive absorption peak near 230 nm, which may be attributed to π ➔ π* transition from the CO bond [26]. The PL spectra mainly cover the blue region from 400 to 500 nm for both OCD and MCD, despite the considerable difference in their sizes (average size OCDs: 11 nm versus average MCDs 78 nm), which confirms that it is the surface defects and surface entities that are dominant in emission properties [42]. The PL quantum yield of OCD is determined as 38% whilst that of MCD is 17%.

PL lifetime measurement was carried out to understand the higher QY of OCD versus MCD. PL decay curves under the excitation of a 370 nm pulsed laser (Fig. S3d) were acquired. The PL decay curves of OCD and MCD can be well fitted with a 3-component exponential decay model (Fig. S3d), suggesting the presence of multiple emission centres. The lifetimes of the three emission components of OCD are found at τ1 1.39 ns (13.76%), τ2 4.80 ns (56.44%) and τ3 11.33 ns (29.8%), respectively; while those of MCD is τ1 1.09 ns (18.71%), τ2 3.49 ns (52.90%) and τ3 8.36 ns (28.39%), respectively. The lifetime results are consistent with a previous report [42,43] which attributes the PL of CDs as arising from an integration of PL components from three types of emission centres, namely, σ*-n and π*-n transitions (emissions from functional groups, corresponding to τ1), π*- π transition (emissions from aromatic core of the CDs, corresponding to τ2) and π*-midgap states-π transitions (emission dominated by the midgap states that are created by functional groups and defects, corresponding to τ3). The much-prolonged τ3 in OCD in comparison to τ3 in MCD, namely 11.33 versus 8.36 ns, suggests the existence of deeper trap states in OCD. This is consistent with the chemical structure analysis, which showed the presence of a surface pyridine N oxide group that is highly electronegative and is unique to OCD. In addition, the fast decay dynamics and high fluorescence intensity indicates the direct bandgap nature of reported CDs [44].

The NO moiety of pyridinic N-oxide functional group can act as an effective electron donor that pushes electrons and the CH terminal of chloroform is electron-poor, the pyridinic N-oxide-rich OCD could show sensing function towards chloroform with its fluorescence as the reporting signal. To demonstrate this point, we also employed MCD suspension for comparison. Figs. 4a and S4 illustrate the concentration-dependent fluorescence change of the OCD and MCD, respectively, in the presence of chloroform.

The introduction of chloroform led to maximum fluorescence enhancement of about 50% for OCD and quenching of about 2% for MCD after only 10 s of exposure, which was therefore defined as the detection time. For OCD the fluorescence intensity exhibits excellent linearity with the response to different chloroform concentrations up to the 1 ppm (Fig. 4b). The fluorescence enhancement data can be fitted by the Stern–Volmer equation [45].
(1)
I 0
I
− 1 =
K sv
C
where K
SV is the Stern–Volmer constant, C is the concentration of CHCl3, I
0 and I the PL intensity of OCD without and with different concentrations of chloroform, respectively. For OCD the fluorescence intensity displayed an excellent linear correlation versus the concentration of chloroform with R2 = 0.949 in the range from 0.0001 to 0.05 pm (Fig. 4b). The PL response in the full concentration range, 0.0001–1 ppm, inset in Fig. 4c, clearly shows two regimes of chloroform sensing actions. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be approximately 3 ppb based on the response plot in 0.1 to 50 ppb concentration range. The LOD is much lower than that provided in an earlier report (Fig. 4d) of 830 ppb limit of detection in aqueous solution [46], and importantly is well below the 250–300 ppb guidelines set out in the Australian and WHO drinking water guideline documents.

The binding between the pyridinic N oxide group and the chloroform is illustrated by observing the interaction of pyridine N oxide (NO; analytical grade) with chloroform (CHCl3). As shown in the UV–Vis spectra in Fig. 4c, when chloroform was added in NO solution, the UV–vis absorbance change, particularly in the range of 200–250 nm, is not simply additive. It indicates binding interaction between NO and CHCl3. Further FTIR analysis on NO and CHCl3 interactions shown in Supporting Information (Fig. S6) confirms this observation, proving the critical role of pyridinic N-oxide group on the OCD for the high sensitivity and selectivity towards CHCl3.

The sensing selectivity of OCD to CHCl3 was firstly evaluated by exposing both OCD and MCD to 1 ppm of various anions, cations and chloroacetic acid (which is also a by-product of chlorine disinfection) as shown in Fig. S7. The PL of MCD was insensitive to all tested analytes, while the PL of OCD was insensitive to most of the analytes except CHCl3. Although some of the analytes such as dichloromethane, magnesium, and ethanol also induced slight PL enhancement of OCD, the enhancement upon interaction with CHCl3 was outstanding. To verify the robustness of its selectivity, a mixture of all chloroacetic acids solutions was also tested in which the result demonstrates that OCD is highly selective towards chloroform.

To further challenge the selectivity of OCD to chloroform, four similar types of THMs, namely chloroform, bromoform, bromodichloroform and dibromochloroform, were tested, shown in Fig. 5C. Besides chloroform, bromodichloroform also induced a level of PL enhancement, though not as strong as chloroform; while bromoform and dibromochloroform do not show much influence on the PL intensity, or slightly quenched PL.

The computational study of the THMs and CD system found that all of the THM molecules bound weakly to the pyridinic N oxide group on the OCD. Here the focus has been drawn to the interaction with armchair edged sensor molecules, as their results most closely correlate to the experimental results in Fig. 5c, suggesting that armchair sensor sites play a deciding role in the difference between the analyte's PL enhancement. However, much of what is mentioned above is apparent in the zigzag edged sensors to a lesser degree, see Fig. (S8 and S9) in the SI for a more in-depth discussion.

Table 1 shows that variation in binding energy varies by less than 0.5 kJ/mol across all tested species (less than the 2.5 kJ/mol for room temperature). Thus, though binding affinity may be a contributing factor, it does not appear to be sufficiently different across the THMs to be considered a critical factor to the PL enhancement effect.

On the contrary, analysis of the molecular orbitals may provide a more illuminating contrast between bromine and chlorine containing THMs. Each molecular orbital is an approximation of a single electron wavefunction. Thus, they show the regions of the molecule in which an electron is most likely to be found. In the case of a photoluminescent system we are interested primarily in the molecular orbitals at or near the HOMO (Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital) and LUMO (Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital) energy levels. This is because, in general, the transition between these levels is the source of the emitted light.

In Fig. 5b i), it can be seen that chloroform has no notable wavefunction overlap with the pyridinic N oxide group on OCD in any of its low-lying excitations, suggesting that no transfer of electrons from CD to chloroform occurs at low lying excitations. From this observation it can be inferred that the bound chloroform molecule could act as a passivating agent, preventing interactions between the solvent molecules and electron density located about the exposed pyridinic oxide group which might lead to non-radiative recombination. This can be compared to bromoform Fig. 5b iii) which exhibits a significant wavefunction overlap for the LUMO, LUMO+1 and LUMO+2 levels. Thereby, an optically excited electron will more frequently be found on the bromoform molecule, allowing electrons to be scavenged by the solvent, interrupting the process of radiative recombination and likely producing less enhancement of photoluminescence. Bromodichloromethane is between these two cases, possessing a small overlap at the LUMO+1 level Fig. 5b ii); thus, it should also have passivation effect, but not as strong as chloroform. These results correspond with those presented in Fig. 5c, with chloroform providing the strongest PL enhancement followed by bromodichloromethane, and then bromoform.

There remains an outlier in this set of results however, that being dibromochloromethane. The computational results show a binding energy between bromoform and bromodichloromethane at −13.7 kJ/mol. Observing the molecular orbitals, it can be seen that there is a significant wavefunction overlap at the first three excitations Fig. 5b iv), similar to bromoform; however, the overlap is of notably lesser magnitude. If the previous arguments are reasonable and hold true, it should be expected that dibromochloromethane would produce an enhancement of the OCD photoluminescence, with a magnitude between bromoform and bromodichloromethane. However, the experimental results in Fig. 5c display a slight PL quenching in the presence of dibromochloromethane (compare with ‘blank’). This conflict between theory and experiment suggests that some unaccounted-for factor may have played a significant role in the interaction of sensor and analyte, such as the molecular dynamics of the solvent, in this case, water. With no current evidence for properties leading to a quenching effect in comparison to the other THMs, it is possible that a deeper level of study is required to ascertain why dibromochloromethane quenches rather than enhances photoluminescence. There are several immediate options to pursue with regards to this dilemma; to move to a higher level of DFT with dispersion corrections e.g. ωB97x-D, change the solvation model (perhaps an explicit model is required), or study the excited-state transitions more directly via time-dependent DFT.

In summary, our current study reveals that human hair as a biowaste is the excellent source material for producing highly fluorescent carbon dots with different surface functionalities, which is determined by the synthesis methods. Conventional autoclaving and microwave fast processing of the same hair sample resulted in two types of CDs with distinctively diverged physicochemical properties. Of note, the species of N-containing functional groups and other functional groups can be tuned by the synthesis method, and the difference results in varied functionalities and sensing performance. In particular, the pyridinic N oxide exhibited on the OCDs has imparted extraordinary chloroform sensing functionality, achieving a LOD of 3 ppb towards chloroform, the highest sensitivity of detection of chloroform in water using CDs reported till date. The computational study confirmed binding between the pyridinic N oxide group and THM molecules. More importantly, the computational work revealed that host-guest binding events lead to different ways of wavefunction interactions, and their effect could be enhancement, quenching, or even no influence, which is essentially determined by their electronic structures. Overall, the study shows that biowaste derived CDs could possess novel functionalities due to inherent elemental doping, which would otherwise be expensive to obtain synthetically, and such functionalities in addition to the PL properties can find important applications such as water quality sensing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that the work described has not been published previously, that it is not under consideration for publication elsewhere, that its publication is approved by all authors and tacitly or explicitly by the responsible authorities where the work was carried out, and that, if accepted, it will not be published elsewhere in the same form, in English or in any other language, including electronically without the written consent of the copyright-holder.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: A.S. acknowledges QUT for the postgraduate research award. The data reported in this work was obtained at the Central Analytical Research Facility (CARF) operated by the Institute for Future Environments at QUT. Prashant Sonar is thankful to QUT for financial support from the Australian Research Council (ARC) for the Future Fellowship (FT130101337) and QUT core funding (QUT/322120-0301/07). Q.L. acknowledges the support of Australian Research Council Industry Transformational Research Hub (IH 180100002). Ehsan Eftekhari acknowledges the support from the SIEF STEM+ Business Fellowship and Griffith University ECR Industry Collaborative Grant. The authors acknowledge the support from the Queensland node of the Australian National Fabrication Facility (ANFF), a company established under the National Collaborative Research Infrastructure Strategy to provide nano-and microfabrication facilities for Australia's researchers. This research was supported by the Griffith University Gowonda HPC Cluster.
